name: Random Forest
summary: Ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples and random feature subsets. Reduces variance and overfitting by averaging individual tree estimators.
use_when: Tabular datasets. High-cardinality feature sets requiring automated importances. Serves as a robust non-linear baseline.
avoid_when: High-dimensional sparse data like text tf-idf or pixel images. Low latency production requirements. Massive scaling where LightGBM/XGBoost are faster.
pros: Resilient to scaling issues, no normalization required
Handles missing values and categorical data structures natively
Outputs impurity-based or permutation-based feature importances
Reduced overfitting risk relative to individual decision trees
cons: Slow real-time inference due to traversing multiple deep trees
High VRAM/RAM footprint for large number of estimators
Lacks extrapolation capabilities beyond the training range boundaries

key_hyperparams.name: n_estimators
key_hyperparams.note: Number of trees in forest. Scales linearly with training/inference latency. Usually stable at 200-500.

key_hyperparams.name: max_depth
key_hyperparams.note: Maximum depth of trees. Defaults to fully grown. Set 8-15 to prune tree branches and reduce noise sensitivity.

key_hyperparams.name: max_features
key_hyperparams.note: Size of random feature subsets at split nodes. 'sqrt' for classification, 0.33 for regression.

key_hyperparams.name: min_samples_leaf
key_hyperparams.note: Minimum data samples required in leaf nodes. Increase to 2-10 to prevent overfitting on outlier nodes.

quick_start:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

clf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    max_depth=12,
    max_features='sqrt',
    min_samples_leaf=2,
    random_state=42,
    n_jobs=-1
)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
importances = clf.feature_importances_
